explanation.text: Riley crouches next to Craig so that he can climb onto her back for a ride. She must bend down to his level, so "crouched" means bent down.

Craig has just said that he needs skates in order to fly, and Riley says nothing on the walk home. A few days later she brings him her old skates, which shows that she had likely been thinking about how to get skates for him.

Once the newspaper makes the skates fit, Craig cannot stop smiling and does not want to take them off even for the walk to the lake. These reactions show that he is very excited to learn how to skate.
Craig inches forward behind the chair, and his blades make a scratching sound instead of his sister's quick swish. Comparing himself to a snail therefore emphasizes how slowly he is skating.

In paragraph 6, carrying Craig makes Riley too slow, so the piggyback ride does not give him the feeling of flight. In paragraph 23, Riley and Liz solve that problem by pulling him fast across the ice on his own skates.

Craig's first attempt at skating is difficult, but help from Riley and Liz finally lets him race over the ice and feel as if he is flying. His joyful new experience supports the message that trying something new can be exciting and wonderful.
Paragraph 5 says lightning heats the air and causes it to expand quickly. That expanding air then creates the booming sound of thunder, so it happens immediately before thunder is heard.

Paragraph 6 says lightning flashes faster than a blink and can travel miles from ground to cloud or between clouds. These details support the main idea that lightning moves very quickly across long distances.

The second illustration shows four labeled forms: forked, ribbon, heat, and sheet lightning. Those different shapes and settings support the idea that lightning can appear in several ways across the sky.
The passage directly explains that nitrogen is a nutrient because it feeds plants and helps them grow. That context shows that a "nutrient" is a form of food or nourishment.

The "Fast Facts" section says lightning places nitrogen in the ground and air, where it feeds plants and helps them grow. It answers how lightning helps the earth because it identifies a useful effect on plants.
The passage states that when lightning strikes a tree or dry grass, a wildfire can begin. This is a cause-and-effect relationship because the lightning strike causes the fire to start.
The crab closes its large claw around the bait and holds on while the narrator raises the string. In this context, "clamped" means grabbed tightly.

The narrator imagines cracking shells and eating the crabs' sweet white meat, then says, "We had to keep trying." This thought best reveals that catching crabs for supper is important and worth the effort to the narrator.
Paulie and the narrator both dislike broccoli casserole, which is what they expect to eat if they return without crabs. They keep trying because catching crabs will let them avoid that unwanted meal.
Before running to the porch, Paulie and the narrator hear scratching and other noises coming from the bait bucket. They go toward the light because they realize that something is moving inside it.

In paragraph 26, the children think their failed crabbing trip means they must eat broccoli casserole. Paragraph 31 solves that problem because the spare-bait bucket unexpectedly contains a large pile of crabs.

The children believe their chance for a crab dinner is gone and start walking home disappointed. Their unexpected discovery of many crabs shows that something good can happen even when hope seems lost.
